EN ES
Minca guides · Layer 1

How Many Days Do You Actually Need in Minca?

By Honey Published: July 21, 2026

At Cacao Cultural they hang a makeshift screen on Friday nights at six thirty and arrange the house chairs into rows that don't quite hold their line. Fifteen people fit, if nobody moves much. A glass of wine comes with the ticket. Beer and something to snack on are for sale at the back.

One of them is a filmmaker and picks the movie. It's never the film you'd expect in a mountain town.

When it ends, nobody gets up right away. Someone says something about the ending, someone else disagrees, and the conversation runs half an hour longer than the film did. The next day, one of the people who was there asks at the front desk whether they can stay another night.

That's the whole answer, but it needs the rest of this to make sense.

If you came to see things, three days

Here's something you won't read anywhere else: Minca doesn't hold that much.

If you arrived with a list, you'll finish it fast. River, waterfall, coffee, sunset, a couple of good restaurants. Three unhurried days covers all of it. The range of things to do here is narrow, and stretching it to five doesn't make it deeper. It just makes it slower.

Some people show up convinced one day is enough, because another traveler put it on their list as a must. They come up at eleven, ask how long the waterfall takes, and by four they're heading back down. They saw a waterfall. It's true that they saw a waterfall.

I've watched those people stay three days. Not because they found more places, but because on the second day they stopped counting them.

If you didn't come to see things

There's another kind of traveler here, and they're not the common one.

They arrive with no plan, book two nights, stretch to a third, then head to Palomino. Two days later they're back asking what a week would cost. They're not on vacation. They already did Peru, they've been six months moving town to town looking for somewhere to stop for a while.

Those people don't come back for the waterfall. They already saw it.

What doesn't show up on lists

Cine 40 isn't in any guidebook. Neither is the conversation afterward.

And that's more or less the logic behind everything that makes someone extend their stay: it can't go on a list, because it isn't a place. It's one specific night with whoever showed up that night. Nobody can promise you that. Neither can I.

What I can tell you is that whoever finds it stops asking how many days Minca needs.

One day

It works. From Santa Marta by moto-taxi or shared van, forty-five minutes if the road is in decent shape, longer if it rained hard the days before.

One day covers a waterfall, lunch in town, and getting back down before dark. Marinka and Pozo Azul lie in different directions out of town, so with limited time, pick one. You don't do both in an afternoon.

What doesn't fit into one day: the mornings. The birds move between five thirty and eight, and that's the real argument for Minca over anywhere else on this coast. If you come up at eleven, it's already over.

The coffee farms higher up don't fit either, or anything that means more than two hours of walking.

Two or three nights

This is what most people do, and it works.

Day one goes to arriving, settling in, and doing nothing. That isn't wasted time. It's the day your body figures out that you walk slower here because it's hot and everything is uphill.

Day two is usually the long one: coffee or a waterfall, or both if you plan well. The coffee farms receive visitors in the morning. La Victoria, the best known and still milling on old hydro-powered machinery, works by advance booking only. Don't go up hoping they'll take you.

Day three depends on who you are. The ones who get up for birds leave before dawn. The ones who don't have a long breakfast and head down mid-morning.

Los Pinos is a real climb, not a stroll. On foot it's three hours up and three back, and there are two routes: one past Marinka, another up the road that runs toward Pozo Azul. You can get most of the way by motorbike, and plenty of people do.

If you want waterfalls that aren't crowded, Cascada Escondida and Cascada El Jaguar are the other two. You reach both on foot, and that's exactly the filter. You earn them with your legs, which is why there are fewer people up there.

If you're heading to or coming from Ciudad Perdida

This is the use of Minca almost nobody explains, and it's one of the most common.

Before the trek, Minca is where you get your legs under you. The walk to Teyuna is a minimum of four hours a day in heat and on grades, and the first day is what breaks people. Going up to Mirador 360 or doing one of the longer routes here, two or three days ahead, completely changes how that first day feels. It won't make you an athlete. It'll spare you the shock.

After the trek, Minca does the opposite. People coming down from Ciudad Perdida arrive with legs that won't hold them, and the town has what they need: cooler air, a river, food, and nobody rushing them. Two nights here on the way down is one of the better itinerary decisions you can make in this region.

One practical thing worth knowing: don't carry the thirty kilos. In Santa Marta, Casa Bonita stores heavy luggage cheaply while you do the trek. They also have rooms with private bathrooms, a fridge, and a shared kitchen, very clean, and Claudina runs the place well. We store bags here too. What you need on Ciudad Perdida fits in a small pack; the rest just weighs on you four hours a day.

Five days or more

Something else opens up after the fourth day, but only if you stop chasing places.

Cerro Kennedy is what comes up when people look for more. It sits around 3,000 meters, and from the top, with luck and clear sky, you can see the snow peaks. Most people leave before dawn by motorbike and come back the same day, or sleep up there for the sunrise. This isn't a guided nature walk. It's cold up top, you need to bring clothes, and the weather decides.

Paso del Mango is on the other side and almost nobody goes. Fewer people, more river.

The rest of those long days don't get filled with activities. They get filled with meeting someone, going back to the same restaurant three times, working out how the town actually runs.

And with the river. Going down to the water mid-afternoon, staying until it cools, coming back up once the fire is lit. That isn't a plan. That's what happens when you stop having one.

There are also people who come for something else. The Sierra has a medicine tradition that didn't start with tourism and still runs outside of it. Those people don't ask how many days they need; they arrive with an open date and fit themselves to what they find. They're not the common traveler, and this piece won't guide them, because whoever is looking for that already knows who to ask.

The power

It goes out without warning and without a schedule. Sometimes briefly, sometimes for most of an afternoon, and although the last few weeks have been steadier, it's still the hard part of living here.

For someone passing through it works differently. No power means no screen, and what's left is walking, getting into the river, talking to strangers. More than one guest has had their best afternoon here because of a blackout.

What helps: a charged battery, a flashlight or the one on your phone, and some cash on you, because card readers don't work without power.

The practical part, no decoration

Paying for things. Almost everyone in Minca takes cards. Most use Bold, a reader that runs off a phone, and locals move money through Nequi. The idea that you have to come up from Santa Marta with all your cash is circulating on blogs nobody has updated. Two real caveats: the card fee runs 5 to 8 percent and some places pass it to you, and without electricity there's no card reader.

ATMs. Two in town. One sits at the junction heading toward Pozo Azul. They can run out of bills in high season.

Getting up here. Shared vans leave Santa Marta's market every half hour, from seven thirty in the morning to five thirty in the afternoon. A moto-taxi is faster and leaves whenever you want. A regular car makes it to town without trouble; above town, many roads are motorbike or 4x4 only.

Luggage. If you're going to or coming from Ciudad Perdida, leave the heavy bag behind. Casa Bonita in Santa Marta stores it cheaply, and we can store it here too.

Sandflies. They bite harder than mosquitoes and come out at dusk near water. Ordinary drugstore repellent does little. Light clothing but long sleeves after five.

Weather. Clear mornings, mist or downpours in the afternoon, especially between April and November. That isn't bad luck, that's how this mountain works. Plan the long things for the morning.

Altitude and temperature. The town sits at 650 meters. Cooler than Santa Marta, not cold. Higher up, past 1,500, you'll want long sleeves at night.

Internet. It works in town and fails higher up. If you're coming to work, ask before you book instead of assuming, and factor in the power cuts.

Season. December through January and Holy Week the town fills up and prices rise. The trails to the waterfalls turn into queues.

Three days if you came to see Minca.

The question of how many days is really the question of what you came to do, and almost nobody answers that before arriving.

Written from Entre Río y Fuego, two rooms above the river in Minca.
